Interpreting the municipal OLS benchmark
Actual OLS results (N=276, R²=0.701, adj-R²=0.694):
The collinearity screener selected these predictors: log_population_density, share_education_higher, share_age_65plus, secondary_residence_share, vacant_total_share, share_artificialized. Note that tourism_beds_density and poi_density_total were not retained in the final predictor set (likely excluded by VIF screening due to their high correlation with population_density and share_artificialized); the coefficient discussion below is restricted to what is actually in the model.

Significant predictors and their interpretation:

share_education_higher (β = 5.538, p < 0.001): the strongest and most significant predictor. A 10-pp increase in higher-education share is associated with exp(5.538 × 0.10) − 1 ≈ 74% higher median price — reflecting the human-capital demand concentration in metropolitan municipalities.
share_age_65plus (β = −5.358, p < 0.001): large and significant negative effect. Municipalities with older demographic structures tend to be rural, economically less dynamic, and experiencing depopulation, depressing housing demand and prices. A 10-pp increase in the 65+ share is associated with approximately −42% in median price.
secondary_residence_share (β = +1.023, p = 0.011): positive and statistically significant in this model. This may seem to contradict the bivariate negative correlation with prices, but once education share and age structure are controlled for, secondary residence positively predicts price. The likely mechanism: controlling for age (low 65+ share in Algarve) and education (low education share absorbed by the large negative age coefficient), the remaining secondary residence variation captures Algarve-style coastal demand, which is positively price-relevant.

Non-significant predictors:

log_population_density (p = 0.170): not significant once education and age are controlled, reflecting multicollinearity with education (r = 0.59). Population density adds little independent price information beyond the human-capital and age-structure covariates in this specification.
vacant_total_share (p = 0.747) and share_artificialized (p = 0.488): both not significant in this run.

Model fit: R² = 0.701 is within the expected range (0.60–0.80) for a national municipal model with 5–6 predictors. The dominant drivers (education, age structure, secondary residence) explain a large share of log-price variation.
Residual map interpretation: after conditioning on the selected indicators, expect two classes of systematic residual pattern: 1. Positive residuals (under-predicted prices) in municipalities near the Lisboa and Porto metropolitan cores — adjacent municipalities benefit from agglomeration spillovers (commuter access, labour market integration) not fully captured by their own indicators. 2. Positive residuals in some Algarve interior municipalities where the tourism-coastal premium is not fully captured by the selected predictors. Any large spatially contiguous cluster of same-sign residuals directly motivates SAR or SEM specifications.

Both Robust LM tests reject simultaneously → General branch → SDM is the first model (Elhorst 2010). The dual-mechanism structure of the Portuguese national housing market — metropolitan demand-displacement contagion (SAR-type) and Algarve regional unobservable (SEM-type) — is consistent with simultaneous rejection of both tests. Note: the code’s diagnostic label reads “Ambiguous robust LM signals” which is a labelling artefact; numerically, both tests are overwhelmingly significant and the “both reject” branch applies unambiguously.

What the code does: Estimates five spatial models (SLX, SAR/SLM, SEM, SDM, SDEM) using the Queen W matrix on the ~270–276 municipality estimation sample. Assembles the model comparison table (log-likelihood, AIC, residual Moran after each model). Identifies the most interpretable predictor (municipal_priority_variable) for WX construction in SDM and SDEM. Displays full coefficient tables for all models.

Interpreting the municipal spatial model comparison
Model selection context: both Robust LM tests rejected in B7 (“General branch”), so SDM is the formal Elhorst (2010) entry model. SAR and SEM are reached from SDM via the LR_θ and Common Factor LR_λ simplification tests, respectively. The comparison table provides the log-likelihoods and AIC values needed to evaluate these tests.

Critical diagnostic finding — SEM and SDEM fail the residual Moran test:
Despite achieving the best AIC (SEM: −41.8) and second-best AIC (SDEM: −40.8), SEM residual Moran = 0.756 and SDEM residual Moran = 0.741 are substantially higher than the OLS residual Moran (0.620). SEM and SDEM did not absorb spatial autocorrelation — they worsened it. This is a fundamental diagnostic failure: the λ process in SEM/SDEM models the spatial correlation of the errors, but it does so in a way that — in this estimation — concentrates the spatial autocorrelation rather than distributing it. The reason is that λ_SEM = 0.848 and λ_SDEM = 0.837 are very large (close to the upper stability bound of 1), which can produce near-singular error covariance matrices and numerical instability.
SAR and SDM successfully absorb spatial autocorrelation: - SAR reduces residual Moran from 0.620 to 0.040 (94% reduction). - SDM reduces it to −0.009 (essentially zero; slight over-correction).
The AIC vs. residual Moran contradiction: the information criteria (AIC/LL) and the residual spatial diagnostics disagree on model quality for this dataset. AIC favours SEM and SDEM; residual Moran favours SAR and SDM. In spatial econometrics, both criteria are informative but serve different purposes. The residual Moran is a more direct test of whether the model has captured the spatial structure of the data — and by this criterion, SAR and SDM are clearly superior. Model selection should weight the residual Moran heavily when the goal is absorbing spatial dependence.
SAR ρ = 0.649: within the expected range for the national scale. The multiplier (1 − ρ)⁻¹ ≈ 2.85 implies that total impacts are roughly 2.85× the direct effects — substantial national cascade effects.
SDM ρ = 0.705, with WX term W_log_population_density: the SDM adds a spatial lag of log_population_density. The θ coefficient for W_log_population_density reflects whether municipalities surrounded by dense neighbours command higher or lower prices after controlling for own-municipality density. This is the key variable-specific spillover that SDM captures and SAR cannot.

Recommended model for Track B: based on the LR_θ result (SDM preferred over SAR) and the residual Moran criterion (SDM near-zero), SDM is the preferred specification. Interpret its coefficients and impacts in section B9.

SLX at the municipal scale: WX coefficients describe whether neighbouring municipalities’ predictor levels influence a focal municipality’s prices beyond what that municipality’s own indicators explain. A positive θ on W_log_population_density (negative in the SDM result: θ = −0.108) suggests municipalities surrounded by dense neighbours have slightly lower prices after controlling for own density — potentially reflecting price competition from alternative supply in adjacent markets.
SDEM is a robustness benchmark. Despite its best AIC among models, SDEM’s residual Moran failure (0.741) disqualifies it as the primary model in this dataset. It serves as evidence that the spatial dependence here is driven by the endogenous spatial lag (ρ), not by correlated errors (λ).

Residual Moran progression:
OLS (0.620) → SLX (0.570) → SAR (0.040) → SDM (−0.009)
The large drop from SLX to SAR confirms that endogenous price contagion (ρ) absorbs the bulk of spatial autocorrelation at the national scale. The further improvement from SAR to SDM confirms that the WX term for log_population_density captures additional spatial structure beyond what ρ alone provides.



2.5.9 B9. Municipal Spatial Impacts
What the code does: Computes direct, indirect, and total LeSage–Pace impacts for SAR (all predictors) and SDM (all predictors, with θ applied only where WX was estimated). Builds the OLS β vs. SAR direct comparison table with multiplier inflation. Displays SEM and SDEM coefficient tables with explicit direct = β, indirect = 0 annotations. Prints ρ estimates for both SAR and SDM.

        y_is_log=muni_y_is_log,
    )
    display_table(municipal_sdm_impacts)
else:
    print("SDM model not available (skipped due to over-parameterisation).")
    municipal_sdm_impacts = pd.DataFrame()

# ── (3) OLS β vs SAR direct impact ───────────────────────────────────────────
print()
print("=" * 70)
print("OLS raw β  vs  SAR direct impact — multiplier distortion")
print("=" * 70)
if not municipal_sar_impacts.empty and "direct_impact" in municipal_sar_impacts.columns:
    muni_ols_betas = {
        row["term"]: row["estimate"]
        for _, row in tidy_statsmodels_coefficients(municipal_ols_result, "OLS").iterrows()
        if row["term"] != "const"
    }
    muni_comparison_rows = []
    for _, row in municipal_sar_impacts.iterrows():
        var = row.get("variable")
        if var is None or pd.notna(row.get("note", None)):
            continue
        muni_comparison_rows.append({
            "variable": var,
            "OLS_beta": round(float(muni_ols_betas.get(var, float("nan"))), 4),
            "SAR_direct": round(float(row["direct_impact"]), 4),
            "SAR_indirect": round(float(row["indirect_impact"]), 4),
            "SAR_total": round(float(row["total_impact"]), 4),
            "rho": round(float(row["rho"]), 4),
        })
    if muni_comparison_rows:
        display_table(pd.DataFrame(muni_comparison_rows))

# ── (4) SEM — β = direct effect; indirect = 0 ────────────────────────────────
print()
print("=" * 70)
print("SEM — coefficient table with explicit impact columns")
print("In SEM: direct_impact = β;  indirect_impact = 0 by construction")
print("=" * 70)
if municipal_sem_model is not None:
    muni_sem_coef = tidy_spreg_coefficients(
        municipal_sem_model, "SEM Queen — Mainland municipalities"
    )
    if not muni_sem_coef.empty:
        muni_sem_coef = muni_sem_coef[muni_sem_coef["term"] != "CONSTANT"].copy()
        muni_sem_coef["direct_impact"] = muni_sem_coef["estimate"]
        muni_sem_coef["indirect_impact"] = 0.0
        muni_sem_coef["total_impact"] = muni_sem_coef["estimate"]
        muni_sem_coef["impact_note"] = "SEM: β=direct; indirect=0 (LeSage–Pace)"
        display_table(muni_sem_coef[[
            "model", "term", "estimate", "direct_impact",
            "indirect_impact", "total_impact", "impact_note"
        ]])

# ── (5) SDEM — β = direct; WX = local spillover; indirect on y = 0 ────────────
print()
print("=" * 70)
print("SDEM — coefficient table with explicit impact columns")
print("In SDEM: β (own-X) and θ (WX) are both direct effects;")
print("         no endogenous lag ⟹ indirect effects on y = 0")
print("=" * 70)
if municipal_sdem_model is not None:
    muni_sdem_coef = tidy_spreg_coefficients(
        municipal_sdem_model, "SDEM Queen — Mainland municipalities"
    )
    if not muni_sdem_coef.empty:
        muni_sdem_coef = muni_sdem_coef[muni_sdem_coef["term"] != "CONSTANT"].copy()
        muni_sdem_coef["is_wx_term"] = muni_sdem_coef["term"].str.startswith("W_")
        muni_sdem_coef["direct_impact"] = muni_sdem_coef["estimate"]
        muni_sdem_coef["indirect_impact"] = 0.0
        muni_sdem_coef["total_impact"] = muni_sdem_coef["estimate"]
        muni_sdem_coef["impact_note"] = muni_sdem_coef["is_wx_term"].map({
            True: "WX: local spillover from neighbour X; indirect on y = 0",
            False: "Own-zone X: direct effect only",
        })
        display_table(muni_sdem_coef[[
            "model", "term", "estimate", "direct_impact",
            "indirect_impact", "total_impact", "impact_note"
        ]])

print(f"\nMunicipal dependent variable: {municipal_dependent_variable}")
print(f"Municipal SAR ρ = {municipal_sar_impacts['rho'].iloc[0]:.4f}" if not municipal_sar_impacts.empty and "rho" in municipal_sar_impacts.columns and len(municipal_sar_impacts) else "")
print(f"Municipal SDM ρ = {municipal_sdm_impacts['rho'].iloc[0]:.4f}" if not municipal_sdm_impacts.empty and "rho" in municipal_sdm_impacts.columns and len(municipal_sdm_impacts) else "")
print(f"SDM WX variables: {municipal_sdm_wx_sources}")

Interpreting the spatial impact decomposition — Track B

Which models produce impacts, and why

Core rule: indirect effects on y exist only when the model contains an endogenous spatial lag ρWy. SEM and SDEM absorb spatial structure in the error term, not in y; no multiplier propagates shocks through the outcome network.




Reading the SAR impacts at municipal scale — what this run found
SAR ρ = 0.649. The spatial multiplier (I − ρW)⁻¹ produces economically large indirect effects. The indirect-to-direct ratio for all predictors equals ρ/(1−ρ) ≈ 1.85, so indirect effects are approximately 1.85× the direct effects for every predictor.

Sign reversal



Key observations from the actual output: 1. SAR direct is not uniformly larger than OLS β. For log_population_density, share_age_65plus, and secondary_residence_share, the SAR direct is smaller in absolute value than OLS. This occurs because the ML estimator of SAR jointly re-estimates all β parameters alongside ρ: the β coefficients in the SAR log-likelihood are not simply OLS β scaled by the multiplier. 2. share_artificialized undergoes a complete sign reversal (OLS: +0.237 → SAR direct: −0.479). This coefficient is not statistically significant in OLS (p=0.488) and changes sign under the SAR ML specification, reflecting multicollinearity instability rather than a genuine spatial mechanism. 3. share_education_higher is the most stable predictor: SAR direct (5.609) ≈ OLS (5.538). With the largest and most significant OLS coefficient, education is robust to model specification. 4. The total national impacts are large: SAR total for share_education_higher = 14.10 — a 10-pp increase in education share is associated, in the SAR system, with exp(14.10 × 0.10) − 1 ≈ 312% higher prices aggregated across all municipalities through the ρ chain. This is an aggregate, not a single-municipality effect.



Reading the SDM impacts at municipal scale
SDM ρ = 0.705 and one WX term: W_log_population_density (θ = −0.108, i.e., being surrounded by denser municipalities is associated with slightly lower own-municipality prices after controlling for own density — possibly a market competition effect).
For log_population_density in SDM: β = 0.101, θ = −0.108, direct = 0.093, indirect = −0.113, total = −0.020. The negative total effect contrasts with the positive direct, showing that the θ-driven negative spatial lag more than offsets the ρ-mediated positive propagation for this specific variable.
For all other predictors (θ = 0), the SDM impacts reduce to SAR impacts with ρ_SDM = 0.705.



SEM and SDEM: explicit zero indirect effects on y
Why SEM indirect = 0: in y = Xβ + u, u = λWu + ε, a change in X_i affects y_i via β. It does not enter any other municipality’s structural equation. The λWu process captures spatially clustered unobservables — the Algarve sub-market premium, metropolitan agglomeration rents, coastal geography — that are autocorrelated in the error term. Correcting for this clustering changes β’s efficiency and standard errors but does not create propagation through the y network. However, as established in B8, SEM residual Moran (0.756) is larger than OLS (0.620) in this run, meaning the SEM λ process does not adequately represent the spatial dependence present in the data. SEM β coefficients are therefore not reliable here.
Why SDEM indirect = 0 on y: in y = Xβ + WXθ + u, u = λWu + ε, WXθ enters y as an observable regressor — a first-order local spillover from neighbours’ X values. A change in municipality j’s predictor raises municipality i’s price by θ × w_ij (a local effect proportional to the spatial weight). This does not propagate further through a multiplier chain. SDEM also fails the residual Moran test in this run (residual I = 0.741).
Which model is most defensible for Portugal? The residual Moran criterion clearly favours SDM (residual I ≈ 0): it absorbs the spatial autocorrelation that SEM and SDEM fail to address. The large ρ (0.705) in SDM confirms that demand-displacement cascades from metropolitan cores are a dominant mechanism in the Portuguese national housing market. The SEM mechanism (regional unobservables) may also be present but is not well-captured by the current estimation, possibly due to the extreme λ estimates near the stability boundary.



Policy interpretation of the total impact at national scale
With 276 municipalities in the system, the aggregate national impact of a localised policy intervention is substantial. For share_education_higher in SAR:

A 10-pp increase in higher-education share in one municipality (e.g., a university expansion in Évora):

Direct effect: SAR_direct × 0.10 = 5.609 × 0.10 ≈ 0.561 log-points ≈ 75% higher own-municipality price.
Indirect effect (distributed): SAR_indirect × 0.10 = 8.495 × 0.10 ≈ 0.850 log-points on average across all 275 other municipalities.
Total: SAR_total × 0.10 = 14.10 × 0.10 — the aggregate of direct and all indirect effects.


This is the central policy implication of spatial econometrics: ignoring spatial interdependence (the OLS baseline) underestimates the full national impact of localised housing-market interventions by the ratio SAR_total / OLS_beta ≈ 14.10 / 5.538 ≈ 2.55.

Interpreting the municipal robustness matrix
The robustness matrix systematically tests whether the spatial dependence finding and model conclusions survive changes in: (1) W specification (Queen, Rook, kNN-4, kNN-6); (2) the price outcome (sales total, sales existing, rent); and (3) whether the extreme top 1 % of price values is excluded (primarily Lisboa, Cascais, Oeiras — the extreme outliers of the national distribution).
Interpreting the results:

Residual Moran consistent across W specs: if all four W specifications produce statistically significant positive residual Moran after OLS, the spatial structure finding is robust to the adjacency definition. This is the expected result given the strong national price gradient.
Elhorst LM entry consistent across W specs: if the joint Robust LM_ρ / LM_λ pattern (i.e., which branch is entered) is the same across Queen, Rook, kNN-4, and kNN-6, the spatial dependence is a structural feature of the national housing market rather than an artefact of the adjacency definition. The LR simplification conclusions — SAR vs. SDM (LR_θ) and SEM vs. SDM (Common Factor LR_λ) — should also be stable across W specs to be credible.
Sales total vs. sales existing: both have near-complete coverage (276 and 274 municipalities). Model conclusions should be highly consistent. Minor coefficient differences are expected because the existing-sales market excludes new developments (concentrated in urban peripheries), which carry a structural price premium from newer building standards and amenities.
Rent as outcome (203 municipalities): if ρ (or λ) and coefficient signs are consistent with the sales model, the spatial mechanism generalises across tenure types. If they diverge — e.g., rent Moran lower, rent ρ smaller — this suggests that the rental market has different spatial integration, consistent with rent regulation, sticky rental prices, or sorting of lower-income households into rental housing predominantly in urban cores rather than spreading across the national system.
Excluding top 1 % of prices: removing the 2–3 most extreme municipalities tests whether the strong spatial dependence finding is driven by the Lisboa/Cascais/Oeiras outliers rather than the structural national pattern. If ρ drops substantially after exclusion, the spatial dependence is partly a leverage effect of extreme observations. If ρ remains large, the finding is genuinely distributed across the national housing system.


Comparing Track A and Track B conclusions:








Dimension
Track A (zones, Aveiro/Ílhavo)
Track B (municipalities, mainland Portugal)




N
~131 zones, ~80–120 after filtering
~276 municipalities for sales (near-complete national)


Scale
Sub-municipal, listing-based aggregation
National, administrative polygon boundaries


Price range
Moderate heterogeneity within Aveiro/Ílhavo metropolitan area
Extreme heterogeneity: €208–€4,340/m²


High-price mechanism
Zone characteristics, listing composition, zone amenity mix
Two structural mechanisms: metropolitan human capital and coastal tourism demand


Key W challenge
Physical barrier (Ria de Aveiro severs natural adjacency)
Size heterogeneity (Alentejo: large rural polygons vs. Minho: small dense polygons)


Key data challenge
MAUP, small-N zones, coordinate quality, listing composition bias
Structural missingness in rent target; secondary residence duality


Expected spatial parameter
Moderate ρ/λ (zone-level price contagion)
Larger ρ/λ (national gradient + metropolitan cascade + Algarve unobservable)


Interpretation
Zone median as sub-municipal housing-market segment
Municipal median as national administrative aggregate



If both tracks show positive, significant residual Moran after OLS, the spatial structure of housing prices operates at both local and national scales simultaneously. This multi-scale spatial dependence is theoretically expected: housing-market integration is a hierarchical process — within a city, zone-to-zone contagion follows neighbourhood comparison; across the country, municipal price diffusion follows demand displacement from metropolitan cores and regional demand shocks from national policy, tourism, and labour-market dynamics.
The two mechanisms differ: Track A spatial dependence reflects primarily the local transmission of price information and amenity diffusion within a geographically bounded urban system. Track B spatial dependence reflects the large-scale demand-displacement cascade from metropolitan cores, the structural duality between human-capital and tourism-driven markets, and spatially clustered unobservables that no single administrative dataset can fully capture.

title: 2  Exploratory Core Spatial Econometric Models
text:
2.6 Limitations
Limitations of this analysis: - Asking prices are not transaction prices. Actual sales prices can differ substantially from listed prices, particularly in the Algarve where negotiating margins are large. - Listings are market-filtered: properties not listed on the observed platforms are absent. The dataset over-represents the active resale market and may underrepresent distressed, institutional, or publicly mediated transactions. - Aggregated municipal indicators hide within-municipality variation (the MAUP problem applies to Track B: the same point data produce different spatial patterns at different aggregation levels). - W is a modelling assumption. All results are conditional on the chosen W and require the robustness checks in B10 for credibility. - Significant spatial impacts are associations, not causal effects. No causal identification strategy is applied in this notebook. - The structural duality between the metropolitan human-capital mechanism and the Algarve tourism mechanism suggests that a single-regime linear spatial model is a simplification. A spatial regime model with separate ρ or β vectors for coastal/metropolitan vs. interior municipalities may be more appropriate for policy analysis. - beds_per_resident and share_tourism_beds_al (the latter with 7 missing values) were flagged in B2; the former is entirely empty and excluded from all models.
